summary:
The Thonburi Kingdom (1767–1782 AD) was the short-lived successor state to the destroyed [[event:2422|Ayutthaya Kingdom]], established by General Taksin on the west bank of the Chao Phraya River opposite modern Bangkok. Taksin was a remarkable figure — the son of a Chinese immigrant father and Thai mother, he rose through military service to become governor of Tak province, and when Ayutthaya fell to the Burmese in 1767, he escaped with a force of cavalry and began the reconquest of Siam. Within seven months of Ayutthaya's destruction, Taksin had rallied enough support to drive the Burmese out and reunify most of Siam, a speed of recovery that astonished contemporaries.

Taksin's reign was characterized by military success and eventual political instability. He successfully repelled Burmese invasion attempts, restored Siamese control over Chiang Mai and the northern territories, extended influence over Cambodia and parts of Laos, and rebuilt trade connections — particularly with Chinese merchants through his own family networks. However, his later years saw increasing signs of instability: he reportedly claimed to be a bodhisattva (Buddhist saint) and had monks flogged for failing to pay him homage, behaviors that alienated the Buddhist establishment and the nobility. In 1782, a coup by General Chakri (later Rama I) ended Taksin's reign; Taksin was reportedly executed (though some accounts suggest he survived in exile). Chakri moved the capital across the river to Bangkok, founding the [[event:3248|Rattanakosin Kingdom]] (Chakri dynasty) that rules Thailand to the present day.
